narrative: As an Asian male applicant with an undergraduate GPA of 3.20—right around the typical level for students in this dataset—your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on past students' data to predict bar exam success on the first try. The model assessed your profile, including your Year 1 and Year 3 school grade deciles, LSAT score, family income in the upper-middle bracket, and your plan to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 90% of similar students. It calculated an 84.2% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, a risk high enough that the university rejected your application to prioritize candidates with stronger predicted odds of passing. This decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school grade decile of 1, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers that year—far below the average decile of about 5.5—and strongly pushed the prediction toward failure by signaling a potential drop-off in academic momentum. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile of 2, also well below the typical 5.7 average, reinforcing concerns about early underperformance compared to others who succeeded on the bar. Your LSAT score of 31, lower than the dataset average of 36.7, added further weight toward the failure prediction, as it deviated from the stronger scores that usually correlate with passing. While your solid GPA and upper-middle family background offered some balance, they weren't enough to offset these top concerns in the model's eyes. Compared to typical students who passed the bar, your grade deciles stand out as notably weaker, which is why they dominated the decision. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male student with clear drive, but a data-driven flag on patterns from past outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above 37, and focus on strategies to improve your upper-year grades, perhaps through tutoring or lighter course loads. Gathering stronger letters of recommendation or relevant internships could also help reframe your profile for other schools. Many students in similar positions have rebounded by addressing these exact gaps—your next application could tell a story of targeted growth. Keep pushing; law school doors reopen for those who adapt.
